serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Burmese fried pork belly roll is approximately 150 g, comprising two to three rolled and fried pork belly slices.

nutrient_source.title: Pork belly, cooked (USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy #10006 adapted for fried preparation); supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods fried pork data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese fried pork belly roll exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork belly cooked data (FDC ID 168289 — Pork, fresh, belly, raw; adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption ~+5 g fat per 100 g) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods fried pork preparations. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. All values are estimates and should be verified against laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on cooked pork / meat-dominant low-carbohydrate dish
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese fried pork belly roll. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate fried meat preparations in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is predominantly fat and protein with minimal carbohydrate (~2.5 g/100 g), placing it firmly in the low-GI category. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (30 × 2.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100 g; ~720 mg per 150 g serving) from fish sauce and soy sauce marinades
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from fish sauce and soy sauce; a single 150 g serving provides approximately 720 mg sodium, representing nearly 31% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) but high total fat and saturated fat content may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: While the low carbohydrate content (~2.5 g/100 g) and low GI of this dish result in minimal acute glycemic impact, the high saturated fat content (~12.5 g/100 g) may contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in moderation and pair it with high-fibre vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g), high total fat (~35 g/100 g), and elevated cholesterol (~85 mg/100 g) from deep-fried pork belly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in saturated fat and cholesterol from deep-fried pork belly, which can raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and consider lower-fat protein alternatives in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: ဝက်သားလိပ်ကြော် ရှယ် (romanized: Wet Tha Lit Kyaw Shay) is a Burmese special fried pork belly roll dish served at Myanmar restaurants in Singapore, including Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. The dish consists of seasoned pork belly that is rolled tightly, marinated in a mixture of garlic, ginger, fish sauce, and soy sauce, then deep-fried until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains tender and juicy. The 'ရှယ်' (shay/special) designation typically indicates a premium or upgraded preparation, often with additional seasoning or a larger portion. Texturally, the dish offers a contrast between the crispy fried outer layer and the rich, fatty pork belly interior. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~395 kcal/100 g) and high in fat (~35 g/100 g), particularly saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g), making it a dish to consume in moderation. Sodium content is elevated (~480 mg/100 g) due to fish sauce and soy sauce in the marinade. The glycemic index is low (~30) owing to minimal carbohydrate content, so acute blood glucose impact is limited. Clinically, the dish poses concerns for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat load. It is not halal due to pork content. It is a popular dish in the Burmese diaspora community in Singapore and is typically served as a main dish alongside steamed rice or Burmese noodle dishes.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese fried pork belly roll exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork belly cooked data adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' as this is a pork-based fried dish, not a grain or starch product. The cuisine tags 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee' in the input appear to be misassigned from another dish and are not reflected in this record. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork belly data (FDC ID 168289) adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption; all values should be verified against laboratory analysis. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly cooked data and are not directly reported for this specific Burmese preparation. 4. This dish contains pork and is not halal or kosher. 5. Burmese cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); the mandatory language for this cuisine is Burmese/Myanmar script, which is captured in the food_name field (ဝက်သားလိပ်ကြော် ရှယ်) and in the romanized_local and aliases fields. G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied by the Burmese-script food name in the input and romanized alias coverage. 6. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA sources as documented above.
